stem: A PTA is performing manual muscle testing of the hip abductors. In which position is the test performed against gravity?
options: Side-lying on the contralateral side — the patient lifts the top leg against gravity
rationale: Hip abductor MMT against gravity: side-lying on the opposite side, lift the top leg toward the ceiling. Gravity-eliminated: supine with leg sliding on a surface.

stem: The sciatic nerve divides into which two nerves at the popliteal fossa?
options: Common peroneal (fibular) nerve and tibial nerve
rationale: The sciatic nerve divides into the common peroneal (fibular) nerve (lateral) and the tibial nerve (posterior) at the popliteal fossa. Common peroneal injury causes foot drop.

stem: Which ligament prevents anterior translation of the tibia on the femur?
rationale: The ACL prevents anterior tibial translation and resists rotational forces. Injury mechanism: non-contact pivoting, deceleration, or hyperextension with valgus force.

stem: Normal ankle dorsiflexion ROM is approximately how many degrees?
rationale: Normal ankle dorsiflexion: approximately 20° (0-20°). Normal plantarflexion: approximately 50° (0-50°). Limited dorsiflexion affects gait (reduced heel strike and stance stability).

stem: Which muscle is the primary hip flexor?
rationale: The iliopsoas (iliacus + psoas major) is the primary hip flexor. The rectus femoris also flexes the hip but is primarily a knee extensor. The sartorius assists but is weaker.

stem: The menisci of the knee serve which primary function?
options: Load distribution, shock absorption, and stability enhancement by deepening the tibial plateau surface
rationale: Menisci: distribute compressive loads (50-70%), shock absorption, joint stability (deepen tibial plateau), lubrication, and proprioception. Medial meniscus is more frequently injured.

stem: Which spinal nerve root is most commonly affected in an L4-L5 disc herniation?
options: L5 nerve root (posterolateral herniation compresses the traversing nerve root)
rationale: L4-L5 posterolateral herniation compresses the L5 nerve root (traversing root). Results in weakness of ankle dorsiflexion (foot drop), EHL, and hip abduction. Numbness in lateral leg/dorsal foot.

stem: The supraspinatus muscle initiates which shoulder movement?
options: Abduction — the supraspinatus initiates the first 15-30° of shoulder abduction
rationale: The supraspinatus initiates shoulder abduction (first 15-30°) and stabilizes the humeral head. Most commonly injured rotator cuff muscle due to impingement under the acromion.

stem: Normal knee flexion ROM is approximately how many degrees?
rationale: Normal knee flexion: approximately 135° (0-135°). Functional requirements: 67° for swing phase gait, 90° for stair ascent, 105° for sit-to-stand, 115° for cross-legged sitting.

stem: Which assessment tool measures functional balance through 14 tasks scored on a 0-4 scale?
options: Berg Balance Scale (BBS) — 14 items, each scored 0-4, maximum score 56
rationale: Berg Balance Scale: 14 tasks (sitting, standing, transfers, reaching, turning, one-leg stance, tandem stance), each scored 0-4. Maximum 56. Score <45 indicates fall risk.

stem: A patient with a meniscal tear demonstrates which clinical sign?
options: Joint line tenderness, positive McMurray test (click/pain with rotation during knee flexion), and possible locking/catching
rationale: Meniscal tear signs: joint line tenderness, positive McMurray (click/pain with tibial rotation during passive flexion), locking/catching, effusion, and pain with deep squatting.

stem: A patient with a grade II MCL sprain has which clinical presentation?
options: Moderate pain, swelling, ecchymosis, and laxity with valgus stress testing (but with a definite endpoint)
rationale: Grade II MCL sprain (partial tear): moderate pain/swelling, laxity with valgus stress (1-2° more than uninvolved) but with a definite endpoint. Grade I: no laxity. Grade III: complete tear.

stem: A patient with iliotibial band (ITB) syndrome has pain at which location?
options: Lateral knee (lateral femoral epicondyle) — pain worsens with running, especially downhill
rationale: ITB syndrome: lateral knee pain at the lateral femoral epicondyle. The ITB snaps over the epicondyle during flexion/extension. Worsens with running (especially downhill), cycling, and stair descent.

stem: A patient with plantar fasciitis reports worst pain at which time?
options: First steps in the morning (start-up pain) — pain at the medial calcaneal tubercle
rationale: Plantar fasciitis: worst pain with first steps in the morning (fascia contracts overnight, then stretches with weight-bearing). Pain at the medial calcaneal tubercle. Improves with warming up.

stem: A patient with a Lachman test demonstrating a soft endpoint has which injury?
options: ACL tear — soft or absent endpoint indicates complete ACL disruption
rationale: Lachman test (most sensitive for ACL): the patient supine, knee at 20-30° flexion. Anterior tibial translation with a soft/absent endpoint indicates complete ACL tear.

stem: Which special test assesses for subacromial impingement of the shoulder?
options: Neer test (passive shoulder flexion with scapular stabilization) and Hawkins-Kennedy test (shoulder flexion to 90° with IR)
rationale: Neer test: passive full shoulder flexion (scapula stabilized). Hawkins-Kennedy: 90° flexion then IR. Pain = positive for subacromial impingement (supraspinatus compression).

stem: A patient with a hamstring strain should avoid which activity during the acute phase?
options: Aggressive stretching and high-speed running — risk of re-injury to healing muscle fibers
rationale: Acute hamstring strain: avoid aggressive stretching, high-speed running, and resisted knee flexion. RICE protocol, gentle isometrics, and progressive loading as healing allows.

stem: A patient with a tibial stress fracture should follow which activity modification?
options: Non-weight-bearing or reduced weight-bearing activities (swimming, cycling) until pain-free, then gradual return to running
rationale: Tibial stress fracture: relative rest (avoid impact activities), cross-training (swimming, cycling), gradual return to running when pain-free (10% rule), and assess contributing factors.

stem: A patient with cervical radiculopathy at C6 demonstrates which motor deficit?
options: Biceps and wrist extensor weakness with decreased biceps reflex
rationale: C6 radiculopathy: biceps and wrist extensor weakness (C6 myotome), decreased biceps reflex (C5-C6), and sensory loss in the thumb and lateral forearm (C6 dermatome).

stem: A PTA is performing gait analysis. During the swing phase, which muscle concentrically contracts to clear the foot?
options: Tibialis anterior (ankle dorsiflexors) to prevent foot drop during swing phase
rationale: Swing phase: tibialis anterior dorsiflexes the ankle for foot clearance. Weakness causes foot drop and steppage gait (excessive hip/knee flexion to compensate for foot clearance).

stem: Which gait deviation is characterized by excessive lateral trunk lean over the stance leg?
options: Trendelenburg gait (compensated) — lateral trunk lean due to hip abductor weakness on the stance side
rationale: Compensated Trendelenburg: lateral trunk lean over the weak hip abductor side during stance. Uncompensated: contralateral pelvis drops without trunk lean. Indicates gluteus medius weakness.

stem: A patient is being taught to use a standard cane. On which side should the cane be held?
options: On the unaffected (contralateral) side to create a wider base of support and reduce load on the affected side
rationale: Cane on the contralateral (unaffected) side: creates a broader BOS, reduces joint loading on the affected side (up to 25% body weight reduction), and normalizes gait pattern.

stem: When teaching stair climbing with a cane, what is the correct sequence going up?
options: Cane first, then affected leg, then unaffected leg
Unaffected leg first, then cane and affected leg together ('up with the good')
rationale: Stair ascent with cane: 'Up with the good' — unaffected leg steps up first (lifting body weight), then the cane and affected leg together. Descent: cane and affected leg first, then unaffected.

stem: A patient with an axillary crutch reports tingling in the hand. What is the likely cause?
options: Excessive pressure on the brachial plexus from resting body weight on the axillary pad (crutch palsy)
rationale: Crutch palsy: compression of the brachial plexus (and radial nerve) from leaning on the axillary pad. The pad should rest 2-3 finger widths below the axilla; weight should be on the handgrips.

stem: A patient using a rolling walker demonstrates a shuffling gait pattern. Which cue helps improve step length?
options: Use visual cues (floor markers at target step length) or rhythmic auditory cues (metronome) to normalize step length
rationale: Shuffling gait improvement: visual cues (floor markers, tape lines at target step length), rhythmic auditory cues (metronome for cadence), verbal cues for heel strike, and conscious effort for larger steps.

stem: A patient with a prosthetic limb demonstrates excessive lateral trunk lean during gait. Which prosthetic component might be causing this?
options: Prosthesis is too short (causing lean toward the prosthetic side) or socket is too wide/loose
rationale: Excessive lateral lean causes: prosthesis too short, socket too wide/loose, weak hip abductors, or insufficient lateral wall support. Refer to the prosthetist for alignment/fit assessment.

stem: A patient needs to learn a three-point gait pattern with crutches. What weight-bearing status does this accommodate?
options: Non-weight-bearing (NWB) or toe-touch weight-bearing (TTWB) on the affected leg
rationale: Three-point gait: both crutches advance with the affected leg, then the unaffected leg steps through. Accommodates NWB or TTWB by keeping weight on the crutches and unaffected leg.

stem: A PTA is applying continuous ultrasound at 1 MHz. What type of tissue heating does this achieve?
options: Deep tissue heating (up to 5 cm depth) for muscles, tendons, and ligaments
rationale: 1 MHz ultrasound: deeper penetration (up to 5 cm), heats muscles, tendons, ligaments, and joint capsules. 3 MHz: superficial (1-2 cm), heats skin, superficial tendons, and scar tissue.

stem: When applying iontophoresis with dexamethasone, which electrode carries the medication?
options: Anode (positive) — dexamethasone is negatively charged (phosphate form), but is delivered from the negative electrode; however, dexamethasone sodium phosphate is repelled from the cathode
rationale: Dexamethasone sodium phosphate is negatively charged and is placed under the cathode (negative electrode) for iontophoresis. Like charges repel, driving the medication into the tissue.

stem: A PTA is performing McKenzie extension exercises with a patient who has a posterior disc herniation. Which exercise is the starting position?
options: Prone lying (passive extension from lying position), progressing to prone on elbows, then prone press-ups
rationale: McKenzie extension progression: prone lying (passive extension) → prone on elbows (more extension) → prone press-ups (maximum extension). Goal: centralize symptoms through sustained extension.

stem: Which electrical stimulation parameter determines whether the muscle contraction is a twitch or a tetanic contraction?
options: Frequency (Hz) — above 20-25 Hz produces a smooth tetanic contraction; below produces individual twitches
rationale: Frequency determines contraction type: <20 Hz = individual twitches, 20-25 Hz = incomplete tetanus, >25-50 Hz = complete smooth tetanus. Higher frequencies (50-80 Hz) used for strengthening.

stem: A patient is performing a Nordic hamstring exercise. What type of contraction is primarily involved?
options: Eccentric — the hamstrings lengthen under load as the body lowers toward the ground
rationale: Nordic hamstring exercise: kneeling, body falls forward while hamstrings eccentrically control the descent. Strong evidence for hamstring injury prevention in athletes.

stem: When performing lumbar stabilization exercises, which muscle is the primary deep stabilizer of the lumbar spine?
options: Transversus abdominis — the deepest abdominal muscle that wraps around the trunk like a corset
rationale: The transversus abdominis (TrA) provides deep segmental stabilization by increasing intra-abdominal pressure and fascial tension. It should activate before trunk movement (anticipatory contraction).

stem: A patient is performing a standing balance exercise on an unstable surface (foam pad). What type of training is this?
options: Proprioceptive/neuromuscular training — challenging balance reactions and ankle proprioception on unstable surfaces
rationale: Unstable surface training: challenges proprioception, balance reactions, and neuromuscular control. Progress from stable → foam → wobble board → single-leg variations.

stem: A PTA is performing joint mobilization at the glenohumeral joint to improve flexion. Which direction of glide is used?
options: Inferior and posterior glide (convex-on-concave rule: glide opposite to restricted movement)
rationale: Glenohumeral flexion (convex humeral head on concave glenoid): inferior glide (primary) and posterior glide improve flexion. The convex-concave rule: convex surface glides opposite to movement.

stem: Which aquatic therapy property supports a patient's body weight in the pool?
options: Buoyancy — the upward force of water that reduces apparent body weight (approximately 90% at neck depth)
rationale: Buoyancy: Archimedes' principle — body is buoyed by a force equal to the weight of water displaced. At neck depth: ~90% body weight reduced. At waist: ~50%.

stem: A PTA is applying a cold pack (cryotherapy) to a patient's acute knee sprain. What are the recommended parameters?
options: 10-20 minutes, with a barrier between the cold pack and skin, applied every 1-2 hours during the acute phase
rationale: Cryotherapy for acute injury: 10-20 minutes (depending on body area and adipose tissue), barrier between pack and skin, every 1-2 hours, and check skin regularly during application.

stem: A patient needs a posterior leaf spring AFO. What condition is this most commonly prescribed for?
options: Mild-to-moderate ankle dorsiflexion weakness (foot drop) — provides dorsiflexion assist during swing phase while allowing plantarflexion
rationale: Posterior leaf spring AFO: thin, flexible plastic that assists dorsiflexion during swing. Allows some plantarflexion at heel strike. Used for mild-moderate foot drop without significant spasticity.

stem: A patient with transtibial amputation needs to be educated about residual limb wrapping. What is the wrapping pattern?
options: Figure-of-8 pattern with pressure greatest at the distal end, decreasing proximally, to shape the limb for prosthetic fitting
rationale: Residual limb wrapping: figure-of-8 pattern, greatest pressure distally (tapers proximally), avoid circular turns (tourniquet effect), rewrap every 4-6 hours, and shape the limb for socket fitting.

stem: Which wheelchair component prevents the chair from tipping backward?
options: Anti-tippers — small wheels or extensions behind the rear wheels that prevent backward tipping
rationale: Anti-tippers: small casters or extensions behind the rear wheels that catch the chair if it tips backward. Essential for new users, wheelie learners, and patients with posterior center of gravity shift.

stem: A patient needs a loftstrand (forearm) crutch. What advantage does it offer over an axillary crutch?
options: Greater freedom of hand use (the cuff stays on the forearm when releasing the handgrip), and no risk of brachial plexus compression
rationale: Lofstrand crutch advantages: forearm cuff allows hand release without dropping the crutch (opening doors), no axillary pressure (no crutch palsy risk), and better for long-term use.

stem: A patient needs a hinged AFO. What is the primary advantage over a solid AFO?
options: Allows controlled ankle dorsiflexion (for stair climbing and sit-to-stand) while still blocking excessive plantarflexion
rationale: Hinged AFO: allows controlled dorsiflexion (tibia advances over foot for stair climbing, sit-to-stand) while blocking plantarflexion with a dorsiflexion stop. More functional gait than solid AFO.

stem: A wheelchair user needs the front casters set correctly. What happens if the casters are set too far forward?
options: Increased tendency for the wheelchair to tip forward, difficulty with wheelies, and reduced turning ability
rationale: Caster position affects stability: casters too far forward = reduced forward stability, easier to tip forward. Casters too far back = increased rolling resistance, wider turning radius.

stem: A patient with a knee orthosis (brace) set at 0-90° can do what?
options: Flex the knee from 0° to 90° but cannot flex beyond 90° or hyperextend — controls ROM within safe limits
rationale: Knee orthosis set at 0-90°: allows flexion from full extension (0°) to 90° of flexion. Blocks hyperextension and flexion beyond 90°. Used post-operatively to protect healing structures.

stem: A patient using a standard walker should advance the walker how far with each step?
options: Approximately one arm's length forward — the patient should stay within the frame for stability
rationale: Walker advancement: one arm's length forward, then step into the walker (affected leg first, then unaffected). Stay within the frame — overreaching reduces stability and increases fall risk.

stem: A PTA documents: 'Patient reports pain 6/10 at rest, 8/10 with activity.' In which SOAP section does this belong?
options: Subjective — patient's self-reported symptoms including pain ratings
rationale: Pain reported by the patient is subjective data. If the PTA observes pain behaviors or measures pain response objectively, that would be objective. Self-report = subjective.

stem: Which abbreviation means 'as tolerated' in physical therapy documentation?
options: a.t. or 'as tol' — as tolerated based on the patient's comfort and ability
rationale: Common PT abbreviations: a.t./as tol = as tolerated, WB = weight-bearing, WBAT = weight-bearing as tolerated, NWB = non-weight-bearing, CGA = contact guard assist.

stem: A PTA is writing a goal: 'Patient will ambulate 200 feet with a rolling walker and standby assist on level surfaces within 2 weeks.' Does this meet SMART criteria?
options: Yes — Specific (ambulation), Measurable (200 ft), Achievable (from current level), Relevant (mobility), Time-bound (2 weeks)
rationale: This goal is SMART: specific (ambulation with RW, SBA), measurable (200 ft), achievable (appropriate progression), relevant (functional mobility), and time-bound (2 weeks).

stem: What does 'FWB' mean in physical therapy documentation?
options: Full weight-bearing — the patient can put all their weight through the affected limb
rationale: FWB = Full Weight-Bearing: 100% weight through the limb with no restrictions. Weight-bearing progression: NWB → TTWB → PWB → WBAT → FWB.

stem: A PTA documents: 'The patient demonstrated improved dynamic balance during functional reaching tasks.' What is missing from this documentation?
options: Objective measurements — how far they reached, level of assist, comparison to prior performance
rationale: Missing: objective data (reach distance, assist level), comparison to prior performance, specific tasks performed, and how improvement relates to functional goals. 'Improved' alone is subjective.

stem: Which documentation practice is required when a patient is seen for the first time by a PTA?
options: Verify the plan of care established by the PT, review the evaluation, and document the initial PTA treatment session with reference to PT goals
rationale: PTA first treatment: review the PT's evaluation and plan of care, verify goals and precautions, document the initial treatment with reference to the POC, and contact the PT if questions arise.

stem: A patient on contact precautions requires PT in the therapy gym. What should the PTA do?
options: Treat in the gym as usual
Ideally treat in the patient's room; if gym use is necessary, disinfect all equipment thoroughly after use and follow contact precautions
rationale: Contact precautions in the gym: treat in-room if possible. If gym is necessary: schedule last, use PPE, disinfect all equipment thoroughly, and clean the area after the session.

stem: A PTA drops a sterile item on the floor during wound care. What should be done?
options: Discard the item and use a new sterile replacement — the item is no longer sterile after contact with the floor
Wipe it off and use it
rationale: Sterile technique: any item that contacts a non-sterile surface is contaminated. Discard and replace with a new sterile item. Maintain the sterile field and proper aseptic technique.

stem: How long should alcohol-based hand rub be applied to the hands for effective hand hygiene?
options: 20-30 seconds — cover all surfaces of the hands until dry
Only until it feels dry immediately
rationale: Alcohol-based hand rub: apply enough to cover all hand surfaces, rub for 20-30 seconds until completely dry. If hands are visibly soiled, wash with soap and water instead.

stem: A patient with C. difficile infection requires which precautions?
options: Contact precautions with soap and water hand hygiene (alcohol-based rubs are NOT effective against C. diff spores)
rationale: C. difficile: contact precautions, and soap and water hand hygiene is required (alcohol-based hand rubs don't kill C. diff spores). Bleach-based disinfection for surfaces.

stem: The proper sequence for donning PPE is:
options: Gown, mask/respirator, goggles/face shield, then gloves (last)
rationale: PPE donning order: gown → mask/respirator → goggles/face shield → gloves (last). Doffing order: gloves → goggles → gown → mask (removed outside the room).

stem: A patient reports that their blood glucose was 50 mg/dL this morning. Should the PTA proceed with exercise?
options: Yes — exercise will help regulate glucose
No — blood glucose <70 mg/dL indicates hypoglycemia; the patient needs to eat and raise glucose before exercising
Yes — low glucose is not a concern
Increase exercise intensity to raise glucose
rationale: Hypoglycemia (<70 mg/dL): do NOT exercise. Have the patient eat fast-acting carbohydrate (juice, glucose tablets), wait 15 minutes, recheck glucose. Exercise only when glucose is 100-250 mg/dL.

stem: A patient's oxygen saturation drops to 85% during exercise. What should the PTA do?
options: Stop exercise immediately, rest the patient, monitor SpO2, apply supplemental oxygen if available, and notify the nurse/physician
rationale: SpO2 <88-90%: stop exercise, rest, monitor, apply supplemental O2 if available/prescribed, and notify medical team. Exercise below 88% risks tissue hypoxia and cardiac complications.

stem: A patient on a beta-blocker medication has which exercise implication?
options: Blunted heart rate response — HR will not rise proportionally to exertion; use RPE (Borg scale) instead of target HR for intensity monitoring
rationale: Beta-blockers blunt HR response to exercise (HR won't reach predicted maximum). Use RPE (Borg scale, target 11-14) or talk test instead of target HR for exercise intensity monitoring.

stem: A patient demonstrates orthostatic hypotension when moving from supine to sitting. What should the PTA do?
options: Allow the patient to sit for 1-2 minutes before standing; monitor BP/HR, and teach the patient to change positions slowly
Return the patient to supine and cancel therapy
rationale: Orthostatic hypotension: allow gradual position changes (sit 1-2 min before standing), monitor vitals, teach slow transitions, ankle pumps before standing, and adequate hydration.

stem: Which clinical sign indicates that a patient may be experiencing a pulmonary embolism during therapy?
options: Sudden onset of dyspnea, chest pain (pleuritic), tachycardia, tachypnea, and possible hemoptysis
Normal vital signs with slight fatigue
rationale: PE signs: sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, tachycardia, tachypnea, anxiety, possible hemoptysis (bloody sputum), and hypotension. This is a medical emergency — call 911.

stem: A patient with a pacemaker is receiving PT. Which modality is CONTRAINDICATED?
options: Therapeutic ultrasound over the pacemaker site and shortwave/microwave diathermy
rationale: Pacemaker contraindications: therapeutic ultrasound over/near the pacemaker, shortwave diathermy, microwave diathermy, and certain electrical stimulation modalities near the device.

stem: A PTA notices a patient's wound has increased redness, warmth, purulent drainage, and the patient has a fever. What should be done?
options: Stop treatment, document findings, and immediately notify the nurse and physician — these are signs of wound infection
Ignore if the patient feels fine
rationale: Wound infection signs: increased redness, warmth, purulent (cloudy/colored) drainage, fever, and pain. Stop treatment, document, and notify the medical team for evaluation and possible antibiotics.

stem: A patient with MS reports that their symptoms worsen significantly after taking a hot bath. This phenomenon is known as:
options: Uhthoff's phenomenon — heat-induced temporary worsening of neurological symptoms in MS
rationale: Uhthoff's phenomenon: heat exposure temporarily worsens MS symptoms (vision, weakness, fatigue) due to decreased nerve conduction velocity in demyelinated fibers. Reversible with cooling.

stem: A patient post-stroke in the Brunnstrom recovery Stage 4 demonstrates which movement capability?
options: Movement begins to deviate from synergy patterns; some isolated joint movements emerge
rationale: Brunnstrom Stage 4: spasticity begins to decline, movement starts to deviate from obligatory synergies, and some isolated movements emerge. Transition from synergy-dependent to voluntary control.

stem: A patient with a cerebellar lesion demonstrates ataxic gait. What are the characteristics?
options: Wide base of support, unsteady and staggering, irregular step length, and poor path deviation control
rationale: Cerebellar ataxic gait: wide base, staggering/unsteady, irregular step length and timing, poor path deviation, and inability to tandem walk. Often accompanied by intention tremor and dysmetria.

stem: A patient with Parkinson's disease is performing amplitude-based training (LSVT-BIG). What is the key principle?
options: Exaggerated large-amplitude movements — training the patient to move BIG to counteract the bradykinesia and hypokinesia of PD
rationale: LSVT-BIG: intensive amplitude-based training using exaggerated large movements. Recalibrates the patient's perception of normal movement size. Evidence-based for PD bradykinesia/hypokinesia.

stem: A patient with a complete C7 SCI can perform which functional activity independently?
options: Independent transfers (triceps for push-up), manual wheelchair propulsion, independent self-care with minimal equipment
rationale: C7 SCI (triceps, wrist extensors, some finger extensors): independent transfers (depression transfers), manual wheelchair, independent self-care with minimal adaptive equipment.

stem: A patient with a vestibular disorder performs the Dix-Hallpike test and experiences nystagmus and vertigo. What type of nystagmus is seen with posterior canal BPPV?
options: Upbeat-torsional nystagmus (beating toward the affected ear) with a latency of 1-5 seconds and fatiguing within 30-60 seconds
rationale: Posterior canal BPPV Dix-Hallpike: upbeat-torsional nystagmus toward the affected ear, 1-5 second latency, duration <60 seconds, fatigable with repetition. Treat with Epley maneuver.

stem: A patient with a TBI demonstrates poor initiation and flat affect. Which brain region is most likely affected?
options: Frontal lobe — controls executive function, initiation, personality, judgment, and emotional regulation
rationale: Frontal lobe damage: poor initiation, flat affect, impaired judgment, personality changes, executive dysfunction, and difficulty with planning/organizing. The 'executive center' of the brain.

stem: A patient with peripheral neuropathy has a steppage gait. What causes this pattern?
options: Ankle dorsiflexor weakness (foot drop) — the patient lifts the knee higher than normal to clear the dropping foot
rationale: Steppage gait: excessive hip and knee flexion during swing phase to clear the foot (drop foot). Caused by ankle dorsiflexor weakness (common peroneal nerve injury, peripheral neuropathy).

stem: A patient recovering from Guillain-Barré syndrome has weakness that is worst distally. What is the expected pattern of recovery?
options: Proximal-to-distal recovery — proximal muscles typically recover before distal muscles
rationale: GBS recovery typically follows a proximal-to-distal pattern (reverse of the ascending weakness). Recovery can take weeks to months; some patients have permanent residual distal weakness.

stem: A patient post-ACL reconstruction at 12 weeks has achieved full ROM and good quad strength. What is the next rehabilitation phase?
options: Progressive strengthening, neuromuscular control, introduction of sport-specific agility and plyometric exercises
rationale: ACL 12 weeks: progress to advanced strengthening, neuromuscular control (perturbation training), and begin sport-specific activities (agility, lateral movements, plyometrics) under supervision.

stem: A patient with a posterior hip replacement (posterior approach) is at 3 weeks post-surgery. Which movement precaution is STILL in effect?
options: Avoid hip flexion >90°, adduction past midline, and internal rotation — typically maintained for 6-12 weeks
All precautions are lifted at 2 weeks
rationale: Posterior approach THR precautions (hip flexion >90°, adduction, IR) typically maintained 6-12 weeks per surgeon. At 3 weeks, all precautions are still in effect.

stem: A patient with a total knee replacement at 6 weeks should have achieved what ROM goal?
options: 0-90° minimum (ideally 0-105°+) — adequate for most functional activities
rationale: TKR ROM goals at 6 weeks: 0° extension to 90°+ flexion (ideally 105°+). Functional requirements: 67° gait, 83° stairs, 93° sitting, 105° rising from a low surface.

stem: A patient with an acute lumbar disc herniation at L5-S1 presents with a positive straight leg raise (SLR) test. What does this indicate?
options: Tension on the L5 or S1 nerve root — the disc herniation compresses the nerve, producing radicular pain with SLR
rationale: Positive SLR: reproduction of radicular symptoms (leg pain in a dermatomal pattern) at 30-70° of hip flexion. Indicates nerve root tension/compression, supporting disc herniation diagnosis.

stem: A patient with a medial epicondylitis (golfer's elbow) should avoid which activity?
options: Resisted wrist flexion and pronation — these stress the common flexor origin at the medial epicondyle
rationale: Medial epicondylitis: inflammation of the common flexor origin. Avoid resistive wrist flexion, pronation, and gripping. Use counterforce strap, activity modification, and progressive eccentric loading.

stem: A patient with a chronic rotator cuff tear has compensatory movement patterns. Which strengthening exercises are MOST important?
options: Scapular stabilization (serratus anterior, lower/middle trapezius) and rotator cuff strengthening (ER, IR) for dynamic shoulder stability
rationale: Chronic RCT rehabilitation: scapular stabilization first (serratus anterior, lower/middle trap for proper scapulohumeral rhythm), then rotator cuff strengthening (submaximal), and movement re-education.

stem: A patient with a newly diagnosed spondylolisthesis at L5-S1 should avoid which exercise?
options: Lumbar extension exercises and activities that increase lumbar lordosis (hyperextension)
rationale: Spondylolisthesis (anterior vertebral slippage): avoid lumbar extension (increases shear force and may worsen slippage). Use flexion-based exercises, core stabilization, and neutral spine training.

stem: A patient post-shoulder arthroscopy for a Bankart repair should avoid which movement?
options: Combined abduction and external rotation (apprehension position) — this position risks re-dislocation
rationale: Post-Bankart repair: avoid the apprehension position (abduction + ER) that recreates the anterior dislocation mechanism. Follow surgeon protocol for ROM progression and strengthening timeline.
